questions.question: An adhesive-bonded aluminum doubler shows a large adhesion failure exactly at the metal-adhesive interface. The manufacturing step most likely at fault is:
options: Autoclave temperature overshoot during cure
Surface preparation — contamination or a skipped etch/anodize and primer step
Using film adhesive instead of paste adhesive
Cutting the honeycomb core against the ribbon direction
questions.explanation: Adhesion at the interface depends on a clean, chemically active surface. Oils, release agents, or a missed etch/anodize and primer leave the adhesive nothing to grip, producing interface disbonds.

questions.question: Two fully cured composite parts are joined with a layer of adhesive in a separate cure cycle. This process is called:
options: Co-curing
Co-bonding
Secondary bonding
Splice forming
questions.explanation: Secondary bonding joins two already-cured parts. Co-curing cures laminate and bond together in one cycle; co-bonding mates a cured part to an uncured one.

questions.question: After a hot bonded repair, a honeycomb panel zone is found with the face sheet domed outward and separated from the core. The classic cause of this 'blown core' condition is:
options: Excessive vacuum during bagging
Moisture trapped in the core cells flashing to steam when heated
Adhesive applied too thickly at the fillets
Over-etching of the face sheet before bonding
questions.explanation: Water that has leaked into core cells expands violently as steam during a hot cure or service heating, pressurizing the cells and blowing the skin off the core — a key reason panels are checked for water ingress before heated repairs.

questions.question: Scanning a metal-to-metal bondline in pulse-echo, you see elevated baseline noise at bondline depth and a weakened, ragged far-side echo, but no single clean reflector. This pattern is most consistent with:
options: A large near-side disbond
Scattered porosity in the adhesive layer
A perfect bond with too little gain
A crushed honeycomb core
questions.explanation: Fine scattered porosity returns many small echoes (raised grass) and attenuates energy crossing the bondline, eroding the far-side echo — unlike a discrete disbond, which gives a strong coherent ring or bondline echo.

questions.question: In pulse-echo over a well-bonded honeycomb face sheet, the skin multiples decay quickly because:
options: The thin skin cannot support multiple echoes at all
Sound energy crosses the adhesive fillets and is scattered and absorbed in the core
The initial pulse masks all the multiples
Aluminum face sheets have unusually high attenuation
questions.explanation: A good fillet bond gives the energy a path out of the skin and into the core cell walls, draining the ring. Over a skin-to-core disbond that path disappears and the free skin rings — in honeycomb pulse-echo, ringing means bad.

questions.question: On a through-transmission C-scan, the signal over a suspect zone is one-tenth of the reference amplitude. The transmission loss is:
options: 10 dB
14 dB
20 dB
40 dB
questions.explanation: ΔdB = 20·log₁₀(10/1) = 20 dB. A ratio of 10:1 in amplitude is always 20 dB; half amplitude is 6 dB and one quarter is 12 dB.

questions.question: A tap test gives a dull thud over a honeycomb panel zone instead of a sharp tick. The physical reason — shared with resonance bond testers — is that:
options: A disbonded skin is free to vibrate differently than a skin damped by the structure behind it
The thud indicates thicker adhesive, which absorbs the tap energy
Sound travels faster in disbonded regions
The core density increases over a disbond
questions.explanation: Both methods sense the change in local vibration response: bonded skin is loaded and damped by the structure, while a disbonded skin vibrates freely, shifting frequency and damping — heard as a thud, or seen as a deflection from the instrument's null.

questions.question: A bond specification states that indications separated by less than their average dimension shall be combined. Two 10 mm disbonds sit 6 mm apart in a zone with a 25 mm single-size limit. You should:
options: Accept both — each is well under 25 mm
Evaluate them as one combined indication spanning roughly 26 mm and disposition against the single-size limit
Record only the larger of the two
Average them to 10 mm and accept
questions.explanation: Proximity rules exist because closely spaced disbonds act structurally as one. Combined per the rule (10 + 6 + 10 = 26 mm), the indication exceeds the 25 mm limit and is dispositioned as rejectable/referred.

questions.question: To size a recorded disbond by the 6 dB-drop method in pulse-echo, you:
options: Increase gain by 6 dB and measure the whole screen
Move the probe until the disbond response falls to half its maximum amplitude and mark that probe position as the flaw edge, repeating around the boundary
Find the position of maximum amplitude and measure the probe diameter
Reduce the pulser voltage by 6 dB and note where the echo disappears
questions.explanation: For reflectors larger than the beam, the edge is taken where the response drops 6 dB (half amplitude) as the beam walks off the flaw; walking this boundary maps the disbond's outline and area for comparison with size and density limits.

questions.question: A disbond exceeding the acceptance limit is repaired by the shop. Before the assembly can be returned to service, the bonded repair must be:
options: Visually checked for adhesive squeeze-out only
Accepted on the repair technician's signature
Re-inspected ultrasonically to the same procedure and acceptance criteria, with the results reported
Exempted from inspection, since new adhesive is assumed sound
questions.explanation: Repairs are field-cured bonds — the least controlled process in bonded construction — so they re-enter the same evaluation loop: standardize on the reference panel, examine, evaluate against the criteria, and document before disposition.
